task_prompt:
Magnolia Gardens provides resident John Marshall with three meals. They are planned out two and a half weeks in advance, as well as two snacks each day. 

Is Magnolia Gardens in compliance with Part 487 of the New York Codes Rules and Regulations? Give me a clear answer back in here, and concisely explain your reasoning.
rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/Tom Buchanan","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim, locate the complaint and moving party's supporting affidavit, then review opposing evidence such as a deposition to determine whether it raises a genuine issue of material fact on key elements (duty, breach, causation). If factual disputes exist on those elements, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and affidavit in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues under civil procedure rules.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, or KY 194A.713 on notice), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</example>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
State statutes for assisted living compliance are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE ABBREV>/ as PDFs (e.g., 194A_700-7205.pdf for Kentucky). Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Reorganization/State Statutes/ to discover available states.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Magnolia-specific agreements reside in /Magnolia Documents/Contracts/; files include General Resident Agreement Template.docx and state-variant versions (e.g., Gardens Residency Agreement Martin Miller v1.docx for California).
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Litigation-related documents, including complaints, strategies, and case files for wrongful death, breach of contract, and assisted living matters, are stored under /Litigation Strategy/ with subfolders such as Assisted Living Litigation in General/ (containing PDFs), Breach of Contract Documents/ (containing complaints and related files), and Wrongful Death Documents/. Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Litigation Strategy/ to discover available files.
</description>
<example>
/Litigation Strategy/ contains Assisted Living Litigation in General/, Breach of Contract Documents/, and Wrongful Death Documents/
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Breach of contract complaints and related filings are stored under /Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents/ with possible subfolders such as Complaints/. Use filesystem_server_list_files on that path to locate original, first amended, and second amended versions.
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents"})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Case-specific wrongful death files (complaints, incident reports, depositions, affidavits, medical logs) are located in subfolders under /Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<resident or case name e.g. Tom Buchanan>/. List files there to find documents like Wrongful Death Complaint.docx or deposition transcripts.
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/Tom Buchanan"})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
New York regulations (e.g., NYCRR Part 487 or Title 18 sections on assisted living) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ as PDFs. Use filesystem_server_list_files on /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ or root-level rules PDFs to locate files covering meals, snacks, or operational requirements.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ contains relevant PDFs for NYCRR compliance checks.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
When multiple residency agreement files exist, verify the selected file matches the task scope (general template vs. state-specific or resident-specific version) before comparison; California RCFE forms may contain 60-day notice language that satisfies a 30-day minimum but is irrelevant to other states.
</description>
<example>
Confirm agreement file name and content scope before mapping to statute (e.g., avoid using CA-specific form for KY compliance check).
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When creating new .docx files with docs_server_docs action="create", the content parameter must be a list of valid block objects (each with a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph"); passing empty objects, empty lists, or structures missing the type tag causes repeated validation errors. Always specify directory and file_name separately.
</description>
<example>
Use content=[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text."}] ; do not pass {} or invalid tags.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For tasks involving identification of objectionable testimony in deposition transcripts under a cited evidence rule (such as NY Rule of Evidence 10.03), locate and read the deposition file (e.g., Nurse Deposition.docx) using sequential read_content calls to access the specific paragraph referenced in the task, then analyze statements for violations like lack of personal knowledge, speculation, or hearsay.
</description>
<example>
Use docs_server_docs to read Nurse Deposition.docx pages until paragraph 8 is located; flag statements in that paragraph that fail the rule's requirements for admissibility.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When checking compliance with a specific regulation (e.g., NY Part 487 on meal planning or similar), confirm the exact statutory text and requirements match the scenario facts (such as advance planning interval or snack frequency) before concluding compliance or non-compliance; search multiple related terms if initial results are narrow.
</description>
<example>
Confirm the regulatory text on "meals" or "snacks" directly addresses the provided operational detail (e.g., planning two and a half weeks in advance) before using it.
</summary>
</memory_item>

**Reasoning:** NYCRR §487.8(b) requires at minimum three meals per day plus one nutritious evening snack (the facility meets or exceeds this with 3 meals + 2 daily snacks). The stated practice of planning only 2.5 weeks ahead falls short of this requirement.

[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/Tom Buchanan","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim, locate the complaint and moving party's supporting affidavit, then review opposing evidence such as a deposition to determine whether it raises a genuine issue of material fact on key elements (duty, breach, causation). If factual disputes exist on those elements, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and affidavit in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues under civil procedure rules.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, KY 194A.713 on notice, or CA CCR Title 22 §87507), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal", "87507") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</example>
</memory_item>

<memory_item>
<description>
State statutes for assisted living compliance are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE ABBREV>/ as PDFs (e.g., 194A_700-7205.pdf for Kentucky). Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Reorganization/State Statutes/ to discover available states.
</description>

/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf

</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Magnolia-specific agreements reside in /Magnolia Documents/Contracts/; files include General Resident Agreement Template.docx and state-variant versions (e.g., Gardens Residency Agreement Martin Miller v1.docx for California).

filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})

</memory_item>

California regulations (e.g., CCR Title 22 sections on residential care facilities for the elderly) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ as PDFs. ## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
When multiple residency agreement files exist, verify the selected file matches the task scope (general template vs. state-specific or resident-specific version) before comparison; California RCFE forms may contain 60-day notice language that satisfies a 30-day minimum but is irrelevant to other states.
</description>
<example>
Confirm agreement file name and content scope before mapping to statute (e.g., avoid using CA-specific form for KY compliance check).
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When creating new .docx files with docs_server_docs action="create", the content parameter must be a list of valid block objects (each with a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph"); passing empty objects, empty lists, or structures missing the type tag causes repeated validation errors. Always specify directory and file_name separately.
</description>
<example>
Use content=[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text."}] ; do not pass {} or invalid tags.
</summary>

For tasks involving identification of objectionable testimony in deposition transcripts under a cited evidence rule (such as NY Rule of Evidence 10.03), locate and read the deposition file (e.g., Nurse Deposition.docx) using sequential read_content calls to access the specific paragraph referenced in the task, then analyze statements for violations like lack of personal knowledge, speculation, or hearsay.
</description>

Use docs_server_docs to read Nurse Deposition.docx pages until paragraph 8 is located; flag statements in that paragraph that fail the rule's requirements for admissibility.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When checking compliance with a specific regulation (e.g., NY Part 487 on meal planning or similar), confirm the exact statutory text and requirements match the scenario facts (such as advance planning interval or snack frequency) before concluding compliance or non-compliance; search multiple related terms if initial results are narrow.
</description>
<example>

</summary>

task_prompt:
Review Dr. Anderson's expert report and analyze whether (1) her opinions as to cause of death are admissible and (2) whether you believe there is a greater than an 80% chance of surviving a summary judgment motion on a crumbling skull rule affirmative defense to the complaint if her report is admitted. The case will be heard in New York. Support your assertions with facts from the case files.  

Provide your answer in a new documents document that you create.
rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/Tom Buchanan","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim including a crumbling skull rule affirmative defense, locate the complaint, moving party's expert report or affidavit (e.g., on cause of death), and opposing evidence such as a deposition; assess admissibility of the expert opinions (qualified expert, reliable basis in case-file facts, not speculation) and whether any evidence raises a genuine issue of material fact on whether the negligence accelerated the pre-existing deterioration. If factual disputes exist on key elements or the defense, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and Expert Witness Opinion Letter.docx in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues on duty, breach, causation, and crumbling skull acceleration under civil procedure rules.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, KY 194A.713 on notice, or CA CCR Title 22 §87507), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal", "87507") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
The crumbling skull rule limits a defendant's tort liability to damages caused by accelerating or worsening a pre-existing condition that was independently deteriorating; the defendant is not responsible for the natural progression of the condition. This contrasts with the eggshell skull rule, which holds the defendant liable for all consequences regardless of the plaintiff's pre-existing susceptibility. In New York wrongful death cases, an admissible expert report on cause of death (qualified expert, grounded in case facts, non-speculative) can support a crumbling skull affirmative defense on summary judgment.
</description>
<example>
Crumbing skull analysis: if expert report demonstrates death would have occurred from independent progression of brittle diabetes and comorbidities, liability limited to any additional harm from negligence.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
State statutes for assisted living compliance are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE ABBREV>/ as PDFs (e.g., 194A_700-7205.pdf for Kentucky). Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Reorganization/State Statutes/ to discover available states.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Magnolia-specific agreements reside in /Magnolia Documents/Contracts/; files include General Resident Agreement Template.docx and state-variant versions (e.g., Gardens Residency Agreement Martin Miller v1.docx for California).
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Litigation-related documents, including complaints, strategies, and case files for wrongful death, breach of contract, and assisted living matters, are stored under /Litigation Strategy/ with subfolders such as Assisted Living Litigation in General/ (containing PDFs), Breach of Contract Documents/ (containing complaints and related files), and Wrongful Death Documents/. Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Litigation Strategy/ to discover available files.
</description>
<example>
/Litigation Strategy/ contains Assisted Living Litigation in General/, Breach of Contract Documents/, and Wrongful Death Documents/
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Breach of contract complaints and related filings are stored under /Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents/ with possible subfolders such as Complaints/. Use filesystem_server_list_files on that path to locate original, first amended, and second amended versions.
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Case-specific wrongful death files (complaints, incident reports, depositions, affidavits, medical logs) are located in subfolders under /Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<resident or case name e.g. Tom Buchanan>/. List files there to find documents like Wrongful Death Complaint.docx or deposition transcripts.
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/Tom Buchanan"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
New York regulations (e.g., NYCRR Part 487 or Title 18 sections on assisted living) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ as PDFs. Use filesystem_server_list_files on /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ or root-level rules PDFs to locate files covering meals, snacks, or operational requirements.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ contains relevant PDFs for NYCRR compliance checks.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
California regulations (e.g., CCR Title 22 sections on residential care facilities for the elderly) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ as PDFs. Use filesystem_server_list_files on /Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ to locate files covering resident agreements or operational requirements.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ contains relevant PDFs for CCR Title 22 compliance checks.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
When multiple residency agreement files exist, verify the selected file matches the task scope (general template vs. state-specific or resident-specific version) before comparison; California RCFE forms may contain 60-day notice language that satisfies a 30-day minimum but is irrelevant to other states.
</description>
<example>
Confirm agreement file name and content scope before mapping to statute (e.g., avoid using CA-specific form for KY compliance check).
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When creating new .docx files with docs_server_docs action="create", the content parameter must be a list of valid block objects (each with a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph"); passing empty objects, empty lists, or structures missing the type tag causes repeated validation errors. Always specify directory and file_name separately.
</description>
<example>
Use content=[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text."}] ; do not pass {} or invalid tags.
</summary>
</memory_item>

[71] ASSISTANT (tool_call): final_answer({"answer":"MEMORANDUM\n\nRE: Analysis of Dr. Elara Anderson's Expert Report re Admissibility of Cause of Death Opinions and Prospects for Crumbling Skull Affirmative Defense on Summary Judgment\nCase: Daisy Buchanan, as Administrator of the Estate of Tom Buchanan, Deceased v. Grove Garden Living (Sup. Ct. Suffolk County, NY)\n\nDate: October 2025\nPrepared for: Defense Counsel, Magnolia Gardens, Inc.\n\nI. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\n(1) Dr. Elara Anderson's opinions as to cause of death are admissible under New York law. She is a board-certified endocrinologist and Clinical Professor specializing in complex geriatric diabetes, her opinions are grounded in the reviewed case-file facts (intake summary, medication logs, EMS report, death certificate), expressed to a reasonable degree of medical certainty, and not speculative.\n\n(2) There is NOT a greater than 80% chance of prevailing on (or \"surviving\" in the sense of the defense prevailing via) summary judgment on the crumbling skull rule affirmative defense, even if the report is admitted. Factual disputes on the causation/acceleration element create triable issues of material fact, making denial of the motion likely under CPLR 3212.\n\nII. DR. ANDERSON'S QUALIFICATIONS AND THE REPORT\nDr. Anderson is Board-Certified in Endocrinology, Fellow of the American College of Endocrinology, and Clinical Professor of Medicine with focus on brittle Type 2 Diabetes in elderly patients. She reviewed: Medical Intake Summary (01/28/2024), MAR and BGL Log (12/2024–03/2025), Grove Garden Emergency Transfer Summary (03/16/2025), Suffolk County EMS Run Report (03/16/2025), and Certificate of Death (03/17/2025). See Expert Witness Opinion Letter.docx, Section I.\n\nIII. ADMISSIBILITY ANALYSIS\nUnder New York evidence rules for expert testimony in medical malpractice and wrongful death cases, an expert must be qualified in the relevant specialty, base opinions on facts in the record, and express them to a reasonable degree of medical certainty without speculation.\n\n- Qualification: Endocrinology board certification and specialized practice in unstable diabetes directly matches the Decedent's primary condition. \n- Factual Basis: Opinions cite specific data from files, e.g., 27-year history of brittle diabetes, PAD and HTN causing \"severe, irreversible microvascular and macrovascular damage,\" BGL >700 mg/dL on 03/16/2025, and the timeline that such derangement \"typically takes days or weeks to develop.\"\n- Non-Speculative: Distinguishes omission as \"at most, a minor contributor\" while the \"severity of the ultimate outcome—death—was determined by his pre-existing, systemic frailty.\" The BGL was \"already at a fatal threshold independent of the 07:00 dose.\" See Report Sections II and III.\n- Conclusion on (1): The opinions are admissible.\n\nIV. FACTS SUPPORTING CRUMBLING SKULL DEFENSE\nThe crumbling skull rule (recognized in NY tort law) holds that a defendant is not liable for the natural progression of a pre-existing condition that was independently deteriorating; liability is limited to any acceleration or worsening caused by the negligence.\n\nSupporting facts from case files:\n- Death Certificate: Immediate cause Hyperosmolar Hyperglycemic State (HHS) due to severe hyperglycemia due to acute diabetic crisis; other significant conditions: Unstable Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus (27-year), PAD, Chronic HTN; Manner of Death: Natural; \"death due to injury? No.\" See Death Certificate Tom Buchanan.docx, Tables.\n- Intake Summary: Admitted with \"brittle/unstable\" 27-year DM requiring QID BGL/insulin, PAD, HTN, obesity, mild cognitive decline; \"High Clinical Risk profile\"; \"Failure to follow the prescribed regimen will likely lead to rapid, unavoidable, and catastrophic decline.\" See MEDICAL INTAKE SUMMARY..., Table 1 and Conclusion.\n- Dr. Anderson Report: \"death ... was primarily caused by the irreversible, natural progression and systemic breakdown of his advanced, unstable chronic diseases, and was clinically unavoidable at that stage.\" \"The omission acted, at most, as a minor contributor.\" \"Facility staff ... acted with immediate professional judgment by initiating the 911/EMS transfer by 09:01.\" See Report Sections II, III, IV.\n- EMS and NP Deposition: Timely discovery and transfer documented; NP Sarah Lee testified omissions presented risk but \"underlying advanced disease state was the foundational vulnerability\" and could not state omission was \"the sole cause.\" See SUFFOLK COUNTY EMS RUN REPORT.docx and Nurse Deposition.docx (pp. 57, 62).\n\nThese facts support that the death resulted from the end-stage natural course of the comorbidities.\n\nV. COUNTERVAILING FACTS CREATING GENUINE ISSUES OF MATERIAL FACT\nDespite the above, multiple facts raise triable disputes on whether the negligence accelerated the deterioration:\n- Medication Log: Multiple \"Omitted\" doses documented, e.g., 02/06/25 11:30 and 21:00 (no initials); 02/07/25 BGL 550 mg/dL (critical, confused/lethargic); 03/15/25 16:30 omitted; 03/16/25 07:00 omitted (no BGL check or insulin); 03/16/25 09:00 unresponsive with BGL 700+. See MEDICATION ADMINISTRATION... LOG.docx, Tables 1-3.\n- EMS Field Impression: \"Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS) secondary to Non-Compliance/Medication Omission. Condition Critical.\" See EMS Run Report.\n- NP Deposition: Acknowledged \"clear risk of worsening hyperglycemia\" from missing 07:00 dose; \"the failure to administer care certainly contributed to the progression of his acute crisis.\" See Nurse Deposition.docx, pp. 55, 62.\n- Complaint Allegations: Repeated failures to administer insulin and monitor BGL as direct and proximate cause of the hyperglycemic crisis, organ damage, coma, and death. See Wrongful Death Complaint.docx, ¶¶24-32, 36-37.\n\nThese create disputes on the key element of whether the omissions accelerated the pre-existing decline or were non-causal.\n\nVI. SUMMARY JUDGMENT ASSESSMENT\nIn New York, summary judgment (CPLR 3212) is granted only if the moving party demonstrates entitlement to judgment as a matter of law with no genuine issues of material fact. The non-movant receives the benefit of all favorable inferences.\n\nWhile Dr. Anderson's report provides strong support for the crumbling skull defense and would likely be admitted, the existence of factual disputes on causation/acceleration (evidenced by logs, EMS impression, and NP testimony contradicting sole natural progression) means a genuine issue of material fact exists. Per analogous guidance, where factual disputes exist on key elements or the defense, the motion is likely denied.\n\nTherefore, even with the report admitted, there is not a greater than 80% chance of prevailing on summary judgment via the crumbling skull affirmative defense. The case would likely proceed to trial on the causation issues.\n\nVII. CONCLUSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS\n(1) Admissible: Yes.\n(2) >80% chance of SJ success on crumbling skull defense: No.\n\nConsider using the report in opposition to any plaintiff motion, for settlement leverage, or at trial to limit damages to any proven acceleration. Supplement with additional expert if needed to address the \"secondary to omission\" narrative in EMS report.\n\nRespectfully submitted,\n[Defense Counsel]","status":"completed"})

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you

  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry

  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<case_name>","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim including a crumbling skull rule affirmative defense, locate the complaint, moving party's expert report or affidavit (e.g., on cause of death), and opposing evidence such as a deposition; assess admissibility of the expert opinions (qualified expert, reliable basis in case-file facts, not speculation) and whether any evidence raises a genuine issue of material fact on whether the negligence accelerated the pre-existing deterioration. If factual disputes exist on key elements or the defense, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and Expert Witness Opinion Letter.docx in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues on duty, breach, causation, and crumbling skull acceleration under civil procedure rules.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, KY 194A.713 on notice, or CA CCR Title 22 §87507), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal", "87507") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</example>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
The crumbling skull rule limits a defendant's tort liability to damages caused by accelerating or worsening a pre-existing condition that was independently deteriorating; the defendant is not responsible for the natural progression of the condition. This contrasts with the eggshell skull rule, which holds the defendant liable for all consequences regardless of the plaintiff's pre-existing susceptibility. In New York wrongful death cases, an admissible expert report on cause of death (qualified expert, grounded in case facts, non-speculative) can support a crumbling skull affirmative defense on summary judgment.
</description>
<example>
Crumbing skull analysis: if expert report demonstrates death would have occurred from independent progression of brittle diabetes and comorbidities, liability limited to any additional harm from negligence.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
State statutes for assisted living compliance are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE ABBREV>/ as PDFs (e.g., 194A_700-7205.pdf for Kentucky). Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Reorganization/State Statutes/ to discover available states.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Magnolia-specific agreements reside in /Magnolia Documents/Contracts/; files include General Resident Agreement Template.docx and state-variant versions (e.g., Gardens Residency Agreement Martin Miller v1.docx for California).
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Litigation-related documents, including complaints, strategies, and case files for wrongful death, breach of contract, and assisted living matters, are stored under /Litigation Strategy/ with subfolders such as Assisted Living Litigation in General/ (containing PDFs), Breach of Contract Documents/ (containing complaints and related files), and Wrongful Death Documents/. Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Litigation Strategy/ to discover available files.

/Litigation Strategy/ contains Assisted Living Litigation in General/, Breach of Contract Documents/, and Wrongful Death Documents/
</summary>

filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents"})

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
When multiple residency agreement files exist, verify the selected file matches the task scope (general template vs. state-specific or resident-specific version) before comparison; California RCFE forms may contain 60-day notice language that satisfies a 30-day minimum but is irrelevant to other states.
</description>
<example>
Confirm agreement file name and content scope before mapping to statute (e.g., avoid using CA-specific form for KY compliance check).
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When creating new .docx files with docs_server_docs action="create", the content parameter must be a list of valid block objects (each with a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph"); passing empty objects, empty lists, or structures missing the type tag causes repeated validation errors. Always specify directory and file_name separately.
</description>
<example>
Use content=[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text."}] ; do not pass {} or invalid tags.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For tasks involving identification of objectionable testimony in deposition transcripts under a cited evidence rule (such as NY Rule of Evidence 10.03), locate and read the deposition file (e.g., Nurse Deposition.docx) using sequential read_content calls to access the specific paragraph referenced in the task, then analyze statements for violations like lack of personal knowledge, speculation, or hearsay.
</description>
<example>
Use docs_server_docs to read Nurse Deposition.docx pages until paragraph 8 is located; flag statements in that paragraph that fail the rule's requirements for admissibility.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When checking compliance with a specific regulation (e.g., NY Part 487 on meal planning or similar), confirm the exact statutory text and requirements match the scenario facts (such as advance planning interval or snack frequency) before concluding compliance or non-compliance; search multiple related terms if initial results are narrow.
</description>
<example>
Confirm the regulatory text on "meals" or "snacks" directly addresses the provided operational detail (e.g., planning two and a half weeks in advance) before using it.
</summary>
</memory_item>

task_prompt:
Magnolia Gardens has Adult Care Home facilities in North Carolina. One of the North Carolina Adult Care Home facilities has 25 residents. It has a 400-square-foot enclosed indoor recreational area in the center of the single building facility. 

Does the recreational area comply with Title 10A, Subchapter 13F, Section .0305 of the North Carolina Administrative Code (10A NCAC 13F .0305)? Provide an answer with a single-sentence explanation.
rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<case_name>","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim including a crumbling skull rule affirmative defense, locate the complaint, moving party's expert report or affidavit (e.g., on cause of death), and opposing evidence such as a deposition; assess admissibility of the expert opinions (qualified expert, reliable basis in case-file facts, not speculation) and whether any evidence raises a genuine issue of material fact on whether the negligence accelerated the pre-existing deterioration. If factual disputes exist on key elements or the defense, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and Expert Witness Opinion Letter.docx in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues on duty, breach, causation, and crumbling skull acceleration under civil procedure rules.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, KY 194A.713 on notice, or CA CCR Title 22 §87507), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal", "87507") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
The crumbling skull rule limits a defendant's tort liability to damages caused by accelerating or worsening a pre-existing condition that was independently deteriorating; the defendant is not responsible for the natural progression of the condition. This contrasts with the eggshell skull rule, which holds the defendant liable for all consequences regardless of the plaintiff's pre-existing susceptibility. In New York wrongful death cases, an admissible expert report on cause of death (qualified expert, grounded in case facts, non-speculative) can support a crumbling skull affirmative defense on summary judgment.
</description>
<example>
Crumbing skull analysis: if expert report demonstrates death would have occurred from independent progression of brittle diabetes and comorbidities, liability limited to any additional harm from negligence.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Environment and Sandbox Facts
<memory_item>
<description>
State statutes for assisted living compliance are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE ABBREV>/ as PDFs (e.g., 194A_700-7205.pdf for Kentucky). Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Reorganization/State Statutes/ to discover available states.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Magnolia-specific agreements reside in /Magnolia Documents/Contracts/; files include General Resident Agreement Template.docx and state-variant versions (e.g., Gardens Residency Agreement Martin Miller v1.docx for California).
</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>

Litigation-related documents, including complaints, strategies, and case files for wrongful death, breach of contract, and assisted living matters, are stored under /Litigation Strategy/ with subfolders such as Assisted Living Litigation in General/ (containing PDFs), Breach of Contract Documents/ (containing complaints and related files), and Wrongful Death Documents/. Use filesystem_server_list_files or get_directory_tree on /Litigation Strategy/ to discover available files.

/Litigation Strategy/ contains Assisted Living Litigation in General/, Breach of Contract Documents/, and Wrongful Death Documents/
</summary>

</description>
<example>
filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Litigation Strategy/Breach of Contract Documents"})
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
Case-specific wrongful death files (complaints, incident reports, depositions, affidavits, medical logs) are located in subfolders under /Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<resident or case name e.g. Tom Buchanan>/. List files there to find documents like Wrongful Death Complaint.docx or deposition transcripts.
</description>
<example>

</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
New York regulations (e.g., NYCRR Part 487 or Title 18 sections on assisted living) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ as PDFs. Use filesystem_server_list_files on /Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ or root-level rules PDFs to locate files covering meals, snacks, or operational requirements.
</description>

/Reorganization/State Statutes/NY/ contains relevant PDFs for NYCRR compliance checks.

California regulations (e.g., CCR Title 22 sections on residential care facilities for the elderly) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ as PDFs. /Reorganization/State Statutes/CA/ contains relevant PDFs for CCR Title 22 compliance checks.

</memory_item>

North Carolina regulations for Adult Care Homes (e.g., 10A NCAC 13F) are stored under /Reorganization/State Statutes/NC/ as PDFs. Use filesystem_server_list_files on /Reorganization/State Statutes/NC/ to locate files for compliance checks on facility features.
</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/NC/ contains relevant PDFs for 10A NCAC compliance checks.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Pitfalls, Edge Cases, and Verification Checks
<memory_item>
<description>
When multiple residency agreement files exist, verify the selected file matches the task scope (general template vs. state-specific or resident-specific version) before comparison; California RCFE forms may contain 60-day notice language that satisfies a 30-day minimum but is irrelevant to other states.
</description>
<example>
Confirm agreement file name and content scope before mapping to statute (e.g., avoid using CA-specific form for KY compliance check).
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When creating new .docx files with docs_server_docs action="create", the content parameter must be a list of valid block objects (each with a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph"); passing empty objects, empty lists, or structures missing the type tag causes repeated validation errors. Always specify directory and file_name separately.
</description>
<example>
Use content=[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text."}] ; do not pass {} or invalid tags.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For tasks involving identification of objectionable testimony in deposition transcripts under a cited evidence rule (such as NY Rule of Evidence 10.03), locate and read the deposition file (e.g., Nurse Deposition.docx) using sequential read_content calls to access the specific paragraph referenced in the task, then analyze statements for violations like lack of personal knowledge, speculation, or hearsay.
</description>
<example>
Use docs_server_docs to read Nurse Deposition.docx pages until paragraph 8 is located; flag statements in that paragraph that fail the rule's requirements for admissibility.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
When checking compliance with a specific regulation (e.g., NY Part 487 on meal planning or similar), confirm the exact statutory text and requirements match the scenario facts (such as advance planning interval or snack frequency) before concluding compliance or non-compliance; search multiple related terms if initial results are narrow.
</description>
<example>
Confirm the regulatory text on "meals" or "snacks" directly addresses the provided operational detail (e.g., planning two and a half weeks in advance) before using it.
</summary>
</memory_item>

task_prompt: We are conducting a compliance review of Magnolia Gardens' General Resident Agreement. We are currently determining whether the General Resident Agreement is in compliance with Kentucky Law. Please review the General Resident Agreement and determine if it is in compliance with Kentucky Revised Statutes (KRS) section 194A.713(8). Please provide a short paragraph in reply to me right here, explaining your reasoning.
rendered_trajectory:
[1] USER: ## Reference cheatsheet — formulas, conventions, environment facts, pitfalls

The block below is a **reference cheatsheet** — like a formula sheet and
a page of environment notes clipped to your desk. It collects reusable
formulas, definitions, conventions, documented behaviours of the tools
and execution surface, output-delivery conventions, and pitfall flags
that earlier agents working structurally similar cases found worth
writing down.

**This is reference material, not instructions.** Treat it the way you'd
treat a formula sheet and a notes page during an exam:

  • You do not "follow" a cheatsheet. You consult it when you need a
    specific fact, and otherwise ignore it.
  • Entries are passive reminders, not rules. None of them command you
    to do anything, and none of them describe the steps for this case.
  • Your own reading of the current task is authoritative. If an entry
    suggests a formula, convention, tool-argument form, or delivery
    channel that conflicts with what this task asks for, follow the
    task.
  • Many entries will be irrelevant to this case. That is expected for a
    cheatsheet. Skip them without comment.
  • Do not change a value, formula, methodology, tool-call shape,
    precision, or delivery form you have arrived at independently just
    because an entry mentions something different. Entries describe what
    was useful on *other* cases.

The cheatsheet exists only to spare you from re-deriving a useful
formula, to remind you of a tool or environment behaviour you might
otherwise re-discover the slow way (for example by hitting an avoidable
error first), or to flag a known trap. Work the current case as you
normally would. Do not cite, quote, or mention the cheatsheet in your
answer or your reasoning.

## Reusable Code and Tool-Call Snippets
<memory_item>
<description>
To extract specific text from a PDF (such as statute sections or legal provisions), first call pdf_server_pdf with action="search" using the key term or citation as query (case-insensitive), then follow with action="read_pages" on the returned page number(s). Use max_results=5 and context_chars=100 for search.
</description>
<example>
pdf_server_pdf({"request":{"action":"search","file_path":"/Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/<pdfname>.pdf","query":"194A.713","case_sensitive":false,"max_results":5,"context_chars":100}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To read multi-page .docx agreements, call docs_server_docs with action="read_content" and page_index (starting at 0) to retrieve paragraphs sequentially; repeat for additional pages as needed. Paths must start with / and end with .docx.
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"read_content","file_path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts/<agreement>.docx","page_index":0}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To create a new .docx document (e.g., a legal memorandum or analysis), call docs_server_docs with action="create", specifying directory (folder path) and file_name (ending with .docx) separately, along with content as an array of valid content blocks (each object must have a "type" field such as "heading" or "paragraph" with required fields populated; empty objects or incorrect parameter names like file_path will fail validation).
</description>
<example>
docs_server_docs({"request":{"action":"create","directory":"/Litigation Strategy/Wrongful Death Documents/<case_name>","file_name":"Memo.docx","content":[{"type":"heading","text":"Title","level":1},{"type":"paragraph","text":"Body text here."}]}})
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
To compute dates, day of week, or time intervals in the sandbox when direct Python execution fails, use code_execution_server_code_exec with a shell date command (e.g., date -d 'YYYY-MM-DD' +%A or similar formatting).
</description>
<example>
code_execution_server_code_exec({"request":{"code":"date -d '2025-08-06' +%A"}})
</example>
</memory_item>

## Solution Strategies for Recurring Task Shapes
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews of assisted living residency agreements against state statutes, first locate and extract the exact statutory requirement(s) from the state PDF, then read the target agreement document(s) page-by-page, and compare each enumerated statutory element against the agreement language for presence or omission. When multiple similar agreement files exist, select the most general or state-matched version rather than a state-specific variant.
</description>
<example>
Locate statute PDF → search/read target section → read agreement → map each statutory item (e.g., notice periods, fee provisions) to agreement text.
</example>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For evaluating a motion for summary judgment on a wrongful death claim including a crumbling skull rule affirmative defense, locate the complaint, moving party's expert report or affidavit (e.g., on cause of death), and opposing evidence such as a deposition; assess admissibility of the expert opinions (qualified expert, reliable basis in case-file facts, not speculation) and whether any evidence raises a genuine issue of material fact on whether the negligence accelerated the pre-existing deterioration. If factual disputes exist on key elements or the defense, the motion is likely denied.
</description>
<example>
Locate complaint and Expert Witness Opinion Letter.docx in litigation folder → read deposition of key witness (e.g., on care protocols) → compare evidence for triable issues on duty, breach, causation, and crumbling skull acceleration under civil procedure rules.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
For compliance reviews against specific regulatory sections (e.g., NY Part 487 on meals/snacks, TX Chapter 276 on deposits, KY 194A.713 on notice, or CA CCR Title 22 §87507), locate the relevant PDF in /Reorganization/State Statutes/<STATE>/ or root-level rules PDFs, use pdf_server_pdf search for key terms (multiple targeted searches if needed), read_pages on matches, and compare provided operational facts against the exact rule language.
</description>
<example>
Locate state PDF → search for regulation keywords (e.g., "meals", "snacks", "pet deposit", "service animal", "87507") → read relevant pages → check if facts satisfy the rule.
</summary>
</memory_item>

## Formulas, Definitions, and Conventions
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(7) and analogous state provisions require a minimum 30-day notice for fee structure changes in assisted living lease agreements; absence of any such notice language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (7): "Minimum thirty (30) day notice provision for a change in the community's fee structure."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(14) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community; absence of such policies constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (14): "Grievance policies that minimally address issues related to confidentiality of complaints and the process for resolving grievances between the resident and the assisted living community."
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
The crumbling skull rule limits a defendant's tort liability to damages caused by accelerating or worsening a pre-existing condition that was independently deteriorating; the defendant is not responsible for the natural progression of the condition. This contrasts with the eggshell skull rule, which holds the defendant liable for all consequences regardless of the plaintiff's pre-existing susceptibility. In New York wrongful death cases, an admissible expert report on cause of death (qualified expert, grounded in case facts, non-speculative) can support a crumbling skull affirmative defense on summary judgment.
</description>
<example>
Crumbing skull analysis: if expert report demonstrates death would have occurred from independent progression of brittle diabetes and comorbidities, liability limited to any additional harm from negligence.
</summary>
</memory_item>
<memory_item>
<description>
KRS 194A.713(8) requires that an assisted living lease agreement include a minimum thirty (30) day move-out notice provision for resident nonpayment, subject to applicable landlord or tenant laws; absence of such language constitutes non-compliance.
</description>
<example>
Statutory element (8): "Minimum thirty (30) day move-out notice provision for resident nonpayment, subject to applicable landlord or tenant laws."
</summary>
</memory_item>

</description>
<example>
/Reorganization/State Statutes/KY/ contains 194A_700-7205.pdf and Regs 902 KAR 20 480.pdf
</summary>
</memory_item>

filesystem_server_list_files({"path":"/Magnolia Documents/Contracts"})
